parts.text: i have run in april 19, in 3days lol if 25km trail run we will run to summit what a agony anyway today should i run?

TLDR: If your 25km summit trail is in 3 days, favor recovery — either rest or a very easy shakeout run (20–30 min) today depending on how you feel. Do not do a hard session.

Why: With only 3 days before a long, hilly trail, you want to preserve glycogen and freshen legs (taper). A short easy run can help loosen up, but anything intense will add fatigue.

Quick guidance
- If you feel fresh, low soreness, and well-rested: do an easy 20–30 minute run at conversational pace + 4–6 short strides (15–20s) to wake the legs. Finish with mobility and light foam rolling.
- If you have any noticeable fatigue, sore legs, niggles, or poor sleep: rest or do a 20–30 minute brisk walk + mobility instead.
- Never do intervals, long tempo runs, or heavy hill repeats within 3 days of the event.

Three-day mini-plan
1. Today (D−3): easy 20–30 min shakeout or rest per above; focus on sleep and hydration.
2. D−2: short easy run 20–25 min OR active recovery (walk, mobility). Eat slightly higher carbs.
3. D−1: mostly rest; optional 10–15 min very easy jog to shake out; pack and final gear check.
Race day: warm up briefly, pace conservatively on climbs (power-hike when needed), fuel early and regularly, prioritize electrolytes.

Practical tips
- Eat a carb-focused dinner the night before; avoid heavy new foods.
- Sleep priority: try for good sleep tonight and the two nights before.
- Practice your fueling plan today if you’re unsure which gels/foods sit well.
- If summit climbs are long, plan to walk powerfully uphill to save legs for flats/downs.

One quick question: when was your last long or hard run (distance/intensity) and do you have any aches/pain right now? That’ll let me tailor the recommendation.
i just run last tuesday 5km 48 mins

TLDR: Yes — but keep it extremely easy. Do a 20–30 min shakeout jog today (or rest if you feel tired/sore). No hard efforts, no hills, and no long runs.

Why (quick): Your only recent run was an easy 5 km on Tuesday (48 min), so you’re not carrying heavy fatigue. With the 25 km summit trail in 3 days you want to preserve freshness while keeping legs moving.

What to do today (choose one)
- If you feel fresh, little/no soreness: easy jog 20–30 minutes at a conversational pace + 4 short strides (15–20 s) after to wake the legs. Finish with 5–10 min mobility and light foam rolling.
- If you’re a bit tired or have any niggle/soreness: rest or do a 20–30 minute brisk walk + mobility.
- Absolutely avoid intervals, hill reps, long tempo runs, or long distances.

Mini taper plan for the next 3 days
1. D−3 (today): shakeout 20–30 min or rest (above).
2. D−2: easy 20–25 min jog or active recovery (walk/mobility). Focus on slightly higher carbs that day.
3. D−1: mostly rest; optional 10–15 min very easy jog to shake out if you feel stiff. Prioritize sleep and hydration.

Race-day tips
- Start conservatively on climbs; power-hike steep sections to save legs for descents.
- Fuel early and regularly (small snacks/gels every 30–45 minutes depending on intensity).
- Carry electrolytes and plan for trail conditions (technical descents need fresh quads).

Quick question: do you have any soreness, injury, or travel/packing planned today that would change whether you can do a short jog?

TLDR: Focus on low‑fiber, easily digestible starchy carbs — rice, pasta, potatoes, oats, bread, bananas, dates, rice cakes — and top up carbs in the 48 hrs before the 25 km. On race day aim for ~30–60 g carbs/hour during the run and a carb+protein snack after.

What to prioritise
- Starches and simple carbs that are easy on the gut: white rice, pasta, potatoes, white bread, bagels, oatmeal, rice cakes.
- Low‑fiber fruit: bananas, ripe melon, peeled apples, dates.
- Concentrated race fuel: energy gels, sports drinks, chews — easy to carry and digest.
- Avoid lots of high‑fat, very high‑fiber, or very spicy foods in the 48 hours pre‑race.

Quick guideline (amounts)
- 48–24 hours before: increase carbs a bit to top up glycogen. Aim for a moderate boost — about 6–8 g/kg/day is common for heavy loading; for a 25 km trail you don’t need extreme loading, so aim at the lower end (roughly 5–7 g/kg/day) if you know your normal intake. If you don’t want math: make carbs ~60–70% of your meals (serve larger portions of rice/pasta/potatoes).
- Night before: a carb‑dominant dinner (see examples) — not huge, but more than usual. Keep fiber moderate.
- Morning of (2–3 hours before): 1–2 g/kg (or a familiar carb meal) — or at least 300–500 kcal from carbs if you don’t want to calculate. If you’re eating only 30–60 minutes beforehand, take 30–60 g easy carbs (banana, toast with honey, or a gel).
- During the run: 30–60 g carbs/hour (start early, every 30–45 min). If you tolerate multiple transportable carbs (glucose+fructose) you can go toward 60 g/hr; otherwise stick to ~30–45 g/hr.
- After the run: 1.0–1.2 g/kg carbs + 15–25 g protein within 30–60 min for recovery.

Practical meal examples
- Today / D−3 (easy day): Oatmeal with banana and honey; or rice with a small portion of lean protein and cooked veggies.
- D−2: Lunch — white rice or pasta with a light sauce and chicken; snack — rice cakes + jam or a couple of bananas.
- Night before: Plain pasta with olive oil or simple tomato sauce and a small piece of chicken/fish; boiled potatoes with a bit of butter; avoid heavy cream or greasy sauces. Dessert: white bread toast with jam or a few dates.
- Morning of (2–3h before): Bagel with honey or jam, banana, and water/tea. If 30–60 min before: 1 gel or 1 banana + sports drink.
- During the run: 1 gel (20–25 g) every 30–45 min or chews/half a sports drink bottle + a small bar/piece of banana as needed.
- After: Chocolate milk or a sandwich + yogurt; e.g., 2 slices white bread + turkey + a small fruit, or 500 ml chocolate milk.

Race fuel suggestions (portable)
- Gels (20–25 g carbs each) — carry 2–4 depending on time.
- Energy chews or blocks.
- Sports drink (mix with water) for both calories and electrolytes.
- Small soft snacks: dates, rice cakes, half a banana in a zip bag.
- Try everything in training first — don’t experiment new foods on race day.

Warnings and tips
- Test your morning and in‑race fuelling in training so you know what your stomach tolerates.
- If you have GI sensitivity, favor liquids and simple sugars (sports drink + small gels).
- Keep sodium/electrolytes in mind if it’s hot or you sweat a lot.
- Prioritize sleep and hydration as much as carbs — they matter for performance.

If you want, tell me your body weight and what foods you normally eat and I’ll give a quick tailored plan with approximate grams and how many gels/snacks to carry.

TLDR: It’s OK once in a while — Jollibee spaghetti + chicken is usable today (D−3) if you keep the portion moderate and avoid extra grease/soda. For D−1 and race morning, prefer cleaner, lower‑fat carbs (plain pasta/rice/potatoes, banana, bagel) and avoid fried chicken.

Details and practical tips
- Spaghetti: Jollibee’s sauce is sweet and fairly high in simple sugars — good for quick carbs. Portion control matters; one normal serving is fine for a pre‑race meal 3+ days out.
- Chicken: if it’s fried (Chickenjoy) it’s high in fat which slows digestion and can leave you feeling heavy. If it’s grilled or you can remove the skin, that’s much better.
- Timing: eat the meal at least 3–4 hours before any hard activity. If you’re running today as a short shakeout (20–30 min), either eat earlier or keep the run to a walk/light jog if you ate recently.
- What to avoid: large portions of fried chicken, greasy sides, milkshakes or large sodas close to activity, and very high‑fiber or spicy sides.
- If you choose Jollibee now: have the spaghetti, take a small piece of chicken (or remove skin), skip the soda (water or sports drink instead), and add a banana later if you want extra carbs.
- Going forward (D−2 and D−1): aim for simpler, lower‑fat carbs — plain pasta/rice/potatoes, bagel with honey, oats — and save fried foods for after the race.

If you want, tell me whether the chicken is fried or grilled and when you plan to run today and I’ll give the exact portion/timing recommendation.
i'm eating brown rice hmmm i just cooke chicken adobo

TLDR: Brown rice + chicken adobo is fine today (D−3) — good carbs + protein — but keep the portion moderate, avoid extra greasy skin/sauce, hydrate, and prefer lower‑fat, lower‑fiber meals in the 48 hours before the 25 km. If you plan to run a shakeout today, wait 1.5–2.5 hours after that meal (or do a short walk instead).

Why and what to do now
- Brown rice gives steady carbs; chicken adobo supplies protein — both useful for recovery and topping up glycogen 3 days out.
- Adobo can be oily and salty. Fat slows digestion and heavy sauce can make you feel sluggish or give GI trouble if you run too soon after eating.
- Practical: remove chicken skin if it’s fried/oily, spoon off excess sauce if possible, and keep your portion reasonable (one typical plate portion, not huge).

Timing guidance
- Small snack only (banana, toast): 30–60 minutes before a short easy run.
- Medium meal (plate of rice + chicken adobo): wait ~1.5–2.5 hours before an easy 20–30 min shakeout.
- Large or very greasy meal: wait 2.5–4 hours or skip the run and walk instead.

Short checklist
- If you’ll run today: either wait 1.5–2.5 hours after eating, or skip the jog and do a 20–30 min walk/very easy mobility session.
- If you’re eating this as dinner tonight (D−3): fine — keep similar meals tomorrow but progressively lower fiber and fat on D−2 and especially D−1 (switch to white rice/pasta, plain bagel, banana).
- Stay hydrated and avoid big sodas/alcohol tonight.
- For race day fueling: plan 30–60 g carbs/hour during the run and a carb+protein snack after (chicken + rice makes a good recovery meal).

Quick question: Do you plan to run today (and if so, when relative to when you ate)? Any stomach sensitivity normally?
